does: FALCON proposes a two-level hierarchical system for improving LLM-based code generation through integrated feedback mechanisms. At the global level, long-term memory retains and applies learned knowledge to enhance code quality across multiple generation attempts. At the local level, short-term memory incorporates immediate compiler and system feedback to refine individual outputs. The system employs meta-reinforcement learning combined with feedback to dynamically adapt during code generation, addressing the failure modes where LLMs struggle to align with precise user intent despite strong instruction-following abilities. The approach specifically targets the inadequacies of standard supervised fine-tuning and RLHF when applied to specialized coding tasks and edge cases.
connects: This work builds on recent advances in using LLMs for code generation and extends prior efforts to improve alignment through human feedback. It responds to known limitations of supervised fine-tuning and reinforcement learning from human feedback in the coding domain, where dataset diversity and specialization remain challenges. The dual-memory architecture draws from cognitive science-inspired approaches and combines insights from long-term learning mechanisms with real-time adaptive feedback loops. The meta-reinforcement learning component connects to broader work on learning-to-learn frameworks that enable systems to quickly adapt to new tasks and feedback signals, extending these ideas to the structured domain of code generation where both semantic correctness and execution validity matter.
enables: FALCON points toward more robust automated programming systems that can handle specialized tasks, domain-specific requirements, and edge cases beyond standard benchmarks. The hierarchical feedback architecture could enable LLM-based code generation systems that learn and improve from deployment experience, accumulating institutional knowledge about common failure patterns. The meta-reinforcement learning approach opens possibilities for code generators that rapidly adapt to project-specific coding standards, libraries, and constraints without full retraining. This work could facilitate development of programming assistants that provide meaningful guidance on why generated code fails and how to fix it, supporting human-AI collaboration in software development and enabling systems that gracefully handle the long tail of programming scenarios.
